questions.text: Which of the following best defines a transmission medium in the context of the physical layer?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A device that converts digital signals to analog.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Anything that can carry information from a source to a destination.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A protocol used for routing packets.

choices.label: D
choices.text: An interface between the data link and network layers.

questions.explanation: According to the text, a transmission medium is anything that can carry information from a source to a destination.

questions.text: Which of the following are categorized as guided media?
questions.explanation: Guided media provide a conduit from one device to another and include twisted-pair, coaxial, and fiber-optic cables.

questions.text: In a twisted-pair cable, what is the primary purpose of twisting the two conductors together?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To increase the physical strength of the cable.

choices.label: B
choices.text: To reduce the effects of noise and crosstalk.

choices.label: C
choices.text: To allow the cable to carry higher voltage.

choices.label: D
choices.text: To distinguish between the signal wire and the ground wire.

questions.explanation: Twisting the wires reduces the effect of external noise or crosstalk from adjacent pairs.

questions.text: What is the function of the second wire in a single twisted-pair connection?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It carries the redundant signal for error correction.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is used for power delivery to the receiver.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is used only as a ground reference.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It carries the clock synchronization signal.

questions.explanation: In a twisted pair, one wire is used for carrying signals, while the other is used as a ground reference.

questions.text: Which statement regarding Shielded Twisted-Pair (STP) cable is true compared to Unshielded Twisted-Pair (UTP)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: STP is thinner and more flexible than UTP.

choices.label: B
choices.text: STP is cheaper to manufacture than UTP.

choices.label: C
choices.text: STP has a metal foil or braided-mesh covering to reduce interference.

choices.label: D
choices.text: STP does not require a ground reference wire.

questions.explanation: STP includes a metal foil or mesh covering that reduces interference, though it makes the cable bulkier and more expensive.

questions.text: In the categorization of UTP cables, what does a higher category number (e.g., Category 7 vs. Category 1) signify?

choices.label: D
choices.text: The use of plastic instead of copper conductors.

questions.explanation: UTP categories are determined by quality, with 1 being the lowest and 7 being the highest.

questions.text: What is the 'local loop' in telephone network applications?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The internal wiring within a central office.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The connection between two different telephone exchanges.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The line that connects subscribers to the central telephone office.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The wireless link used by cellular phones.

questions.explanation: The local loop is the specific twisted-pair line connecting a subscriber to the central telephone office.

questions.text: What does the term 'gauge' refer to in the context of UTP cable performance?

choices.label: B
choices.text: The measure of the thickness of the wire.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The number of twists per inch.

questions.explanation: Gauge is a standard measure of the thickness of the wire conductor.

questions.text: Which characteristic allows coaxial cable to carry higher frequency ranges than twisted-pair cable?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The use of two ground wires.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Its physical construction, including a central core and an outer shield.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The lack of insulation on the central core.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The fact that it is only used for analog signals.

questions.explanation: Coaxial cable's design allows it to carry higher frequency ranges than twisted-pair cables.

questions.text: In a coaxial cable, the outer conductor serves two functions. What are they?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Shielding against noise and completing the circuit as a second conductor.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Transmitting light and reflecting electromagnetic waves.

questions.explanation: The outer conductor acts as a shield against noise and as a second conductor to complete the electrical circuit.

questions.text: Coaxial cables are categorized by which of the following ratings?
questions.explanation: Coaxial cables are categorized by Radio Government (RG) ratings, which denote unique physical specifications.

questions.text: What is the purpose of a BNC terminator in a coaxial network?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To branch the connection to a computer.

choices.label: B
choices.text: To amplify the signal at the end of the line.

choices.label: C
choices.text: To prevent the reflection of the signal at the end of the cable.

choices.label: D
choices.text: To convert the signal from analog to digital.

questions.explanation: A BNC terminator is used at the end of a cable to absorb the signal and prevent it from reflecting back.

questions.text: Compared to twisted-pair cables, which of the following is a characteristic of coaxial cable performance?
questions.explanation: Coaxial cable has much higher attenuation than twisted-pair cable, causing the signal to weaken rapidly and necessitating frequent repeaters.

questions.text: In many modern telephone networks, coaxial cable has largely been replaced by which medium?
questions.explanation: The text notes that fiber-optic cable has largely replaced coaxial cable in modern digital telephone networks.

questions.text: Fiber-optic cables transmit signals in the form of:
questions.explanation: A fiber-optic cable is made of glass or plastic and transmits signals in the form of light.

questions.text: What is the relationship between the core and the cladding in a fiber-optic cable?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The cladding is denser than the core.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The core is made of metal, while the cladding is glass.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The core is surrounded by a cladding of less dense glass or plastic.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The cladding is used to carry the primary signal.

questions.explanation: In fiber optics, the core is surrounded by a cladding of less dense material to allow for internal reflection.

questions.text: In multimode graded-index fiber, how does the density of the core change?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It remains constant from center to edge.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is lowest at the center and increases toward the edge.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is highest at the center and decreases gradually toward the edge.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It fluctuates randomly to scatter light.

questions.explanation: In graded-index fiber, density is highest at the center of the core and decreases gradually to its lowest at the edge.

questions.text: Which propagation mode is characterized by a core with a very small diameter and low density (index of refraction)?
questions.explanation: Single mode fiber has a much smaller diameter and lower density, resulting in a critical angle close to 90 degrees.

questions.text: Optical fibers are defined by the ratio of which two physical properties?
questions.explanation: According to Table 7.3, fiber types are defined by the ratio of the diameter of their core to the diameter of their cladding.

questions.text: Which fiber-optic connector is commonly used for cable TV?
questions.explanation: The Subscriber Channel (SC) connector is typically used for cable TV.

questions.text: What is a major performance advantage of fiber-optic cable over metallic cables like copper?
questions.explanation: Fiber-optic cables are immune to electromagnetic interference because they use light rather than electrical signals.

questions.text: Which of the following is considered a disadvantage of optical fiber?
questions.explanation: A disadvantage of optical fiber is that light propagation is unidirectional; two fibers are needed for bidirectional communication.

questions.text: The strength of a fiber-optic cable's construction often comes from which material, also used in bulletproof vests?
questions.explanation: Kevlar is used in fiber-optic cable construction for strength.

questions.text: Unguided media transport electromagnetic waves without using a physical conductor. This is referred to as:
questions.explanation: Unguided communication is commonly referred to as wireless communication.

questions.text: Which propagation method involves signals hugging the curvature of the earth and traveling through the lowest portion of the atmosphere?
questions.explanation: Ground propagation involves radio waves hugging the earth through the lowest part of the atmosphere.

questions.text: In sky propagation, radio waves radiate upward and are reflected back to Earth by which layer?
questions.explanation: Sky propagation relies on waves being reflected back to Earth by the ionosphere.

questions.text: What is a requirement for line-of-sight propagation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The waves must reflect off the ionosphere.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Transmitting and receiving antennas must be directional and face each other.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The signal must be below 3 kHz.

questions.explanation: In line-of-sight propagation, antennas must be directional and facing each other, and they must be high enough to overcome the curvature of the earth.

questions.text: Radio waves are defined as electromagnetic waves ranging in frequency between:
questions.explanation: Radio waves range from 3 kHz to 1 GHz.

questions.text: What is an advantage of radio waves being omnidirectional?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Sending and receiving antennas do not have to be aligned.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They provide high data rates for digital communication.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They cannot penetrate walls, allowing for private communication.

questions.explanation: The omnidirectional nature of radio waves means antennas do not need to be physically aligned.

questions.text: Which frequency band is typically used for microwaves?
questions.explanation: Microwaves range from 1 GHz to 300 GHz.

questions.text: Unlike radio waves, microwaves are unidirectional. This implies that:

choices.label: B
choices.text: They can propagate in all directions simultaneously.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The sending and receiving antennas must be aligned.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They are susceptible to significant interference from other users.

questions.explanation: Microwaves are unidirectional, so transmitting and receiving antennas must be aligned.

questions.text: Which application primarily uses radio waves for multicast communication?
questions.explanation: Radio waves are used for multicast communications such as radio, TV, and paging systems.

questions.text: Microwaves are commonly used for unicast communication. Which of the following is an example?
questions.explanation: Microwaves are used for unicast communication like cellular phones and satellite networks.

questions.text: What is the frequency range for infrared signals?
questions.explanation: Infrared signals range from 300 GHz to 400 THz.

questions.text: Why can infrared signals NOT be used effectively outside a building?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They are absorbed by the atmosphere too quickly.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The sun's rays contain infrared waves that cause interference.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They require a physical conduit to travel long distances.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They cannot travel in a line-of-sight path.

questions.explanation: Infrared cannot be used outside because the sun's rays contain infrared waves that interfere with the signals.

questions.text: Which type of unguided medium is most appropriate for a TV remote control operating within a single room?
questions.explanation: Infrared is used for short-range communication in closed areas (like a room) using line-of-sight propagation.

questions.text: In the context of antennas, a parabolic dish is usually associated with:
questions.explanation: Parabolic dishes (and horn antennas) are types of unidirectional antennas used for microwaves.

questions.text: Which characteristic is shared by both microwaves and infrared signals?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Both are used for long-distance sky propagation.

questions.explanation: The text states that microwaves cannot penetrate walls, and infrared also cannot penetrate walls.

questions.text: What does a BNC T-connector do in an Ethernet network?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Stops the signal at the end of the cable.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Connects the end of a cable to a single TV.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Branches out to create a connection to a computer or device.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Amplifies the frequency of the signal.

questions.explanation: BNC T-connectors are used in Ethernet networks to branch out a connection to a computer or other device.

questions.text: The MT-RJ connector for fiber-optic cables is designed to be the same size as which standard electrical connector?
questions.explanation: MT-RJ is a fiber connector that is the same size as the RJ45 connector used for twisted-pair cables.

questions.text: Which guided medium has the highest immunity to tapping (security)?
questions.explanation: Fiber-optic cables offer greater immunity to tapping, making them more secure than copper-based media.

questions.text: Which of these is NOT a guided medium application according to the text?
questions.explanation: Satellite networks are listed under unguided (wireless) media, specifically using microwaves.

questions.text: In fiber optics, what happens to light rays when the angle of incidence is greater than the critical angle?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The light is refracted into the cladding.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The light is reflected back into the core.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The light is absorbed by the Kevlar jacket.

questions.explanation: When light travels from a more dense to a less dense medium and the angle is greater than the critical angle, it is reflected (Total Internal Reflection).

questions.text: What is the primary disadvantage of using radio waves inside a building?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They cannot penetrate walls, so you lose signal in different rooms.

choices.label: B
choices.text: You cannot isolate the communication to just inside the building.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They require extremely tall antennas for indoor use.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They are easily absorbed by plastic insulation.

questions.explanation: Because radio waves penetrate walls, a disadvantage is that you cannot isolate communication within a building; it may leak outside.

questions.text: How many repeaters are typically needed for fiber-optic cables compared to metallic cables over the same distance?
questions.explanation: The text states that fiber-optic cables require '10 times less' repeaters due to lower attenuation.
